data.title: Diesel margins, cash diffs near 1.5-year highs on support from west
data.description:
Asia's diesel and cash differentials surged to near 1.5-year highs, breaking past a few sessions of limited price fluctuations, reflecting the strength in sentiment especially from the West amid fresh Russian sanctions despite a lack of supply-demand fundamental shifts.

ICE gasoil futures remained firmly propped up as a knee-jerk reaction to a new European Union sanction package on Russia and overnight inventory draws in the northwest Europe region, despite tepid demand.

Asian paper markets rose, especially for August, reflecting the robust trading sentiment for front-month discussions.

The front-month diesel east-west (LGOAEFSMc1) jumped back to discounts of $50-52 a metric ton, with northwest European markets climbing at a faster pace than Asian prices.

On the trading window, deals emerged for the first time this week at much higher premiums as a reflection of the steeper market backwardation, with cash differentials (GO10-SIN-DIF) jumping to $1.89 a barrel - its highest level since early February last year.

For jet fuel, the front-month east-west price spread remained wide in most parts of the week, maintaining above discounts of $43 per ton, according to data from several trade sources. July-loading volumes on the east-west route will likely stay brisk, they added.

Despite the strength in east-west arbitrage trade flows, regrade (JETREG10SGMc1) widened from a week earlier as gasoil markets proved stronger and the spread hit its widest.

SINGAPORE CASH DEALS

- Two gasoil deals, no jet fuel deals

INVENTORIES

- Gasoil stocks held independently in the Amsterdam-Rotterdam-Antwerp (ARA) refining hub fell by nearly 5% on the week to their lowest since December 2023, data from Dutch consultancy Insights Global showed on Thursday.

REFINERY NEWS

- India's state-run Oil and Natural Gas Corporation ONGC.NS is exploring building a 200,000-240,000 barrels per day refinery at Jamnagar in the western Indian state of Gujarat, a company source said on Thursday.

NEWS

- Brazilian state-run oil firm Petrobras may redirect the oil it sells to the United States, sending more to Asia and Pacific markets due to higher tariffs the U.S. announced on Brazil, its chief executive told Reuters on Thursday.

- China's exports of refined products, including diesel, gasoline, aviation fuel and marine fuel, fell 0.6% year-on-year to 5.34 million tons in June, according to the customs data, though the figure marked the highest monthly total since June 2024.

- Oil prices rose on Friday after the European Union agreed to new sanctions against Russia, also underpinned by supply concerns following drone attacks on northern Iraqi oilfields and tight market fundamentals.

data.title: Truist Financial Posts 2Q Miss on Earnings, Revenue
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Truist Financial posted higher second-quarter results, but missed expectations on both the top and bottom lines.

The Charlotte, N.C., bank on Friday posted a profit of $1.24 billion, or 90 cents a share, in the quarter ended June 30, compared with $922 million, or 62 cents a share, a year earlier.

Stripping out certain one-time items, adjusted per-share earnings were 91 cents, behind the 93 cents forecast by analysts, according to FactSet.

Revenue rose to $4.99 billion. Analysts surveyed by FactSet forecast revenue of $5.01 billion.

Interest income fell to $6.15 billion from $6.35 billion. Total earning assets rose to $480.98 billion from $474.14 billion.

Truist is still on track to meet its annual expense growth target, which includes investments in talent and technology, Chief Executive Bill Rogers said.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Wave of short liquidations drives ETH past $3,600 as analyst calls price shift a ‘regime change’
data.description:
Ethereum continued its momentum heading into the weekend, leaping more than 20% this week to trade above $3,600 on Friday after a wave of short liquidations and bullish option bets pointed to a possible run at $4,000 by the July 25 expiry, according to onchain options venue Derive.xyz.

Dr. Sean Dawson, Derive’s head of research, said traders “are aggressively positioned for a rapid move to $4K” and called the spike “a regime change” for ether. About $136 million in ETH shorts were liquidated over 24 hours, Coinglass data shows, fueling a squeeze that pushed prices higher.

Roughly 25% of Derive’s ether volume since Wednesday has centered on $3,000–$4,000 call options expiring July 25, while 8% of all open interest on the platform for that date now sits at the $4,000 strike. Dawson put the probability of Ether finishing July above $4,000 at 14%, rising to 27% for above $5,000 by the end of 2025.

“BTC is participating, but this rally belongs to ETH. The technical setup, option flows, and liquidations all point to a meaningful structural shift in positioning,” he said.

Market elevated, but not yet euphoric

Regulatory tailwinds supported the uptrend. The total crypto market value reached $4 trillion for the first time as bipartisan support advanced the GENIUS Act to the president’s desk and sent the Clarity Act to the Senate, measures that investors view as paving the way for clearer U.S. crypto rules.

Wall Street's appetite for crypto funds remained “extremely strong” following the news. Ethereum especially logged massive flows to reinforce its leadership in this leg of the rally, BRN’s lead research analyst, Valentin Fournier, told The Block. Spot Bitcoin ETFs pulled in approximately $523 million on Thursday, slightly less than $602 million in net inflows to their Ether counterparts, The Block’s data dashboard shows.

Fournier warned that early signs of an overheating market flashed, as new highs will likely encourage broad profit-taking. Selling pressure may trigger crypto volatility in the near term, especially for altcoins. However, the analyst expects institutional flows and legislative clarity to keep the broader up‑trend intact.

“Despite the sharp gains, the Fear & Greed Index sits at 71/100, suggesting investor sentiment is elevated but not yet euphoric,” Fournier said. “We remain overweight on altcoins, particularly Ethereum and Solana, given their outsized performance and institutional traction.”

Meanwhile, Bitcoin consolidated below $120,000 and hit the +1 Standard Deviation above the short-term holder cost basis, which has historically acted as resistance, Glassnode noted. Still, BTC’s sideways price action traded just shy of its all-time high, and data indicates that the next resistance level is higher, at around $136,000, leaving room for more upside.

Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. data.title: AM Best lifts outlook on Canada’s Farm Mutual Re to positive
data.description:
(The Insurer) - AM Best has revised its outlook on Farm Mutual Reinsurance Plan to positive from stable.

The rating agency affirmed Farm Mutual Re's financial strength rating at B-plus-plus, citing improved underwriting performance and stronger earnings.

The outlook revision reflects continued favourable reserve development and the success of a strategic plan launched in 2020 to restore profitability after years of catastrophe-related losses.

The Ontario-based reinsurer posted record net income of C$105.2 million ($76.7 million) last year, benefiting from disciplined underwriting, rate hikes, improved risk selection and tighter expense management, AM Best said in a statement.

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

Late Thursday, President Trump called for the release of additional documents related to the investigation of convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. His directive followed the publication of a WSJ article about a letter bearing Trump's name that was included in a 2003 birthday album for the disgraced financier. Separately, if you buy your own health insurance, expect to pay a lot more next year. Obamacare insurers are asking for hefty premium hikes.

Today's Headlines

In an attempt to tackle the housing crisis, two congressmen are set to unveil a bill to make it easier for homeowners to finance tiny homes on their properties.

Israel's demands for a demilitarized zone south of Syria's capital and protection of the Druze minority are drawing it deeper into conflict.

A pattern of high-value weapons seizures shows Iran is making new efforts to arm its militia allies across the Middle East.

Local governments in China have tried mostly in vain to lift the country's shrinking birthrate. Now Beijing is stepping in.

Live From The Markets

Follow our live financial coverage all day.

The loss of an independent Fed could matter not just for the U.S. economy, but other countries' economies, too.

Economists this month dialed back their earlier pessimism that U.S. trade policies would lead to slower growth and higher inflation this year.

Trophy art is proving to be more sensitive to interest-rate hikes than even sophisticated investors expected, writes Carol Ryan.

Read It Here First

Trump called for the release of 'pertinent' Epstein documents following a WSJ article.

Trump, in a Thursday night social-media post, said he had directed Attorney General Pam Bondi to "produce any and all pertinent Grand Jury testimony, subject to Court approval!" Bondi wrote on X that she was ready to ask a court today to unseal the grand jury transcripts. In a separate social-media post, Trump threatened to sue the Journal and News Corp, the parent company of Dow Jones, for publishing an article about a letter bearing his name, part of a collection made for Epstein's 50th birthday, calling it "false, malicious, and defamatory."

OpenAI CEO Sam Altman outfoxed Elon Musk to become Trump's AI buddy-in-chief.

After Trump's re-election, Musk's position as "first buddy" kept Altman out of meetings at Mar-a-Lago and in the overflow room at the inauguration. So Altman bided his time, quietly maneuvering around his rival to forge AI infrastructure deals and build his own relationship with Trump, report Keach Hagey, Dana Mattioli and Josh Dawsey. Musk's abrupt departure from Washington has left an open lane for Altman to secure government support for a huge global AI infrastructure build-out he needs — and to have more influence over AI regulation.

Expert Take

Q: Where can new college graduates find jobs?

Researchers at payroll-services provider ADP crunched public cost-of-living data and ADP payroll data for more than 5 million U.S. workers in their 20s to see where new college graduates are landing jobs. Their findings show that it pays to look at cities just beyond America's biggest metro areas, Ray A. Smith and Haley Zimmerman report.

A: In one of the toughest markets for entry-level jobs in years, several second-tier cities — including Raleigh, N.C. — rise above the pack for their strong hiring, decent salaries and affordability. Instead of Atlanta or Chicago, consider Birmingham, Ala., or Milwaukee, Wis. Or think Baltimore, in lieu of Washington, D.C.

Those alternative cities, along with Austin, Texas, rank as the top five most promising locations to find work for recent college graduates, according to ADP's new study.

Researchers ranked 55 metro areas on three metrics: affordability, wages and hiring activity. They then weighed hiring rates for jobs that typically require a degree against affordability-adjusted wages in each city to devise a single, overall ranking.

What sets the top-ranking cities apart? Raleigh, Baltimore and Austin — all of which scored in last year's Top 5, too — tend to have a higher-than-usual concentration of technology, health and financial firms, says Ben Hanowell, ADP Research's director of people analytics. Like Birmingham, they are home to big research and healthcare institutions that do a lot of hiring. Milwaukee's job market, meanwhile, is benefiting from the expansion of Eli Lilly and other companies in the area.

See The Story

In China's dark factories, robots run the show.

Skyrocketing robotization in China has led to the spread of what are known as dark factories — so automated that, in theory, the lights could be completely shut off. Robots help to build electric cars around the clock, transforming the Chinese EV industry into an existential threat to American and global automakers.

Happening Today

The Commerce Department releases new residential construction data for June at 8:30 a.m.

The University of Michigan's preliminary July consumer survey is out at 10 a.m ET.

Earnings: American Express, Charles Schwab, 3M, Schlumberger

Number Of The Day

27%

The 2026 rate increase that Blue Cross & Blue Shield of Illinois wants for its Affordable Care Act marketplace plan, the coverage known as Obamacare. Its sister Blue Cross plan in Texas is asking for 21%. The companies say the big increases are needed because of higher healthcare costs and changing federal policy, including cuts to subsidies that help consumers pay for plans.

And Finally. . .

Water sport or crime? The bitter fight over wave-making boats.

Speed boats designed to produce a wave so tall that a person can surf without a rope are rocking waters across Wisconsin and beyond. Dozens of towns in the state are banning wakesurfing, which critics say traumatizes kayakers and tears up lakes. Wakesurfing enthusiasts blame much of the conflict on irresponsible wakesurf-boat drivers and misinformation.

data.title: Chevron Moves Forward With Megadeal for Hess After Winning Exxon Arbitration — WSJ
data.description:
By Collin Eaton

An arbitration panel has cleared the way for Chevron to close its $53 billion purchase of Hess, dismissing Exxon Mobil's claim that it had a contractual right to bid for Hess's crown-jewel assets in Guyana.

The ruling handed down Friday from the International Chamber of Commerce in Paris resolves an often tense and long-running dispute between the two largest descendants of John D. Rockefeller's Standard Oil monopoly over one of the world's most coveted oil projects. Chevron plans to move swiftly to close the deal, which it had originally struck in October 2023.

Exxon threw a wrench in Chevron's plans last year when it asserted a right to pre-empt its rival's bid for Hess's 30% stake in Guyana's prolific Stabroek offshore block. Chevron had argued a right-of-first-refusal on the Guyana project wouldn't apply to the corporate takeover of Hess.

Exxon's move to block the deal stunned the oil industry, which hadn't seen titanic oil companies battle to these extremes since a court fight with Pennzoil forced Texaco into bankruptcy in the 1980s.

Exxon said in a statement that it disagreed with the ICC panel's interpretation but respects the arbitration and dispute resolution process.

data.title: Market Chatter: Meta's WhatsApp Faces Potential Exit from Russia as Government Pushes State Messaging App
data.description:
Meta Platforms' WhatsApp faces potential exit from Russia, Reuters reported Friday, citing Anton Gorelkin, deputy head of the lower house parliament's IT committee.

Gorelkin said it's time for WhatsApp to prepare to leave, noting Meta's designation as an extremist organization in Russia, the report said.

Last month, Russia's President Vladimir Putin signed a law promoting a state-backed messaging app integrated with government services, as Moscow seeks to reduce reliance on foreign platforms like WhatsApp and Telegram, it added.

Meta's Facebook and Instagram have been banned in Russia since 2022, following the invasion of Ukraine.

Meta didn't immediately reply to MT Newswires' request for comment

data.title: Social Buzz: Wallstreetbets Stocks Mostly Higher Pre-Bell Friday; Opendoor Technologies, Robinhood Markets to Advance
data.description:
The most-talked-about stocks in the Reddit subforum Wallstreetbets were mostly higher hours before Friday's opening bell.

Robinhood Markets was up 3.1% in pre-bell hours, after a 2.1% rise at Thursday's close.

Opendoor Technologies was 3% higher pre-bell, following a 10.7% rise from the previous session.

Webull advanced by 2.7% in premarket activity, after closing Thursday with an 11.9% increase.

Lucid Group rose by 1.3% pre-bell, following a 36.2% increase from the previous session.

Rocket Lab was up 0.6% hours before market open, after a 7.6% increase from Thursday's session.

Nvidia was 0.5% higher pre-bell, after a 1% rise from the previous session.

UnitedHealth Group was slightly higher premarket, swinging from a 1.5% decline at Thurday's close.

Netflix was down 1.7% in pre-bell hours, swinging from a 1.9% increase at Thursday's close.

data.title: Berenberg Notes Intermediate Capital Group's 'Solid' Fiscal Q1
data.description:
Intermediate Capital Group logged "a solid start to the year" as shown in the asset manager's fiscal first-quarter trading update, according to analysts at Berenberg.

"Q1 was a solid quarter of fundraising for the business ($3.4bn raised in Q1 compared to H1 consensus of $5.6bn), in our view," the Thursday note said. "We continue to think that ICG is well positioned to continue to scale up its business given its established brand, diversified business model and size."

The stock was confirmed at buy with a price target of 26 pounds sterling.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Rises. CEO Huang Met China's Commerce Minister — and It Went Well. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Elsa Ohlen

Nvidia stock rose in premarket trading Friday after CEO Jensen Huang met with Chinese Commerce Minister Wang Wentao Thursday, in a sign it might deepen ties with China further.

Shares were up 0.5% to $173.84, bringing weekly gains to 5.4%. If gains hold, it would be the fourth straight day of the stock closing at a new all-time high.

S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures were both up 0.1%.

Following a bumpy first half of the year, Nvidia became the first company to ever close with a market capitalization over $4 trillion earlier this month. Shares are up 84% from its 52-week low of $94.31 on April 4 after President Donald Trump announced sweeping global tariffs.

On Thursday, Wang Wentao said that China's market is huge and that he hoped that multinational companies including Nvidia would provide Chinese customers with high-quality products, according to a translation of a Ministry statement.

The Ministry added that Huang said the Chinese market is very attractive and that Nvidia is willing to deepen cooperation with Chinese partners in the field of artificial intelligence.

It comes just days after the chip maker said it expects to soon be able to sell its H20 AI chip in China again, amid easing trade tension between the world's two largest economies.

Write to Elsa Ohlen at elsa.ohlen@barrons.com
